results.content:
Deliverable Data Item: Consists ... product. Demonstration: Showing that the use of an end product achieves the individual specified requirement (verification) or stakeholder expectation (validation)......
Deliverable Data Item: Consists of technical data, such as requirements specifications, design documents, management data plans, and metrics reports, that have been identified as items to be delivered with an end product. Demonstration: Showing that the use of an end product achieves the individual specified requirement (verification) or stakeholder expectation (validation).
AADL Architecture Analysis and Design Language AD2 Advancement Degree of Difficulty Assessment AIAA American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics AO Announcement of Opportunity AS9100 Aerospace Quality Management Standard ASME American Society of Mechanical Engineers ASQ American Society for Quality CAIB Columbia Accident Investigation Board CCB Configuration Control Board CDR Critical Design Review CE Concurrent Engineering or Chief Engineer CEQ Council on Environmental Quality CERR Critical Event Readiness Review CHSIP Commercial Human Systems Integration Processes CI Configuration Item
Life Cycle Cost (LCC): The total of the direct, indirect, recurring, nonrecurring, and other related expenses both incurred and estimated to be incurred in the design, development, verification, production, deployment, prime mission operation, maintenance, support, and disposal of a project, including closeout, but not extended operations. The LCC of a project or system can also be defined as the total cost of ownership over the project or system’s planned life cycle from Formulation (excluding Pre–Phase A) through Implementation (excluding extended operations).

Systems Engineering Processes and Requirements. The systems engineering products covered in this · Handbook are Concept of Operations (ConOps), Requirements, and Verification and Validation. This Handbook contains sections on model planning, setting up the model including model
Engineering Processes and Requirements. The systems engineering products covered in this · Handbook are Concept of Operations (ConOps), Requirements, and Verification and Validation · (V&V). This Handbook contains sections on model planning, setting up the model including
The system modeling method in this Handbook is tool-agnostic. The modeling approach selected · leverages NASA modeling practices but does not reflect all NASA modeling methods. If readers · have their own modeling approach, they can use the metamodel to trace back to their modeling · approach to generate ConOps, Requirement, and Verification and Validation products. ... Software verification processes include analysis, evaluation, review, inspection, assessment, and testing of software products during each phase of the software life cycle (see the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook 273 for an explanation of these verification activities). The IEEE Std 1012, Standard for Software Verification and Validation209, also has a good overview and definition of the different software verification tasks that can be executed on particular software classes as needed).
The choices for software verification activities are dependent upon the software requirements (see SWE-050 and SWE-051), the software architecture (see SWE-057) and design (see SWE-056 and SWE-058), the method of component and system integration (see SWE-060 and SWE-063), and the overall testing philosophy and approach (see SWE-062 and SWE-066 and SWE-073). The software verification engineer gains an understanding of these portions of the software development activities prior to developing the plan for software verification. In addition, the software verification engineer coordinates planning with the software validation planning activities (see SWE-029) to achieve the most efficient and integrated verification activities.
They may also reduce the work effort to develop verification plans by using a plan template. Click here to view master references table. ... results.description: Examples of validation methods ... testing against mathematical models, analyses, and operational environment demonstrations. Refer to the software plan requirements for software validation planning and incorporation (Chapter 5 [of NPR 7150.2, NASA Software Engineering Requirements, section 5.1.1]) [detailed in SWE-102 in this Handbook]...
results.content:
Examples of validation methods ... testing against mathematical models, analyses, and operational environment demonstrations. Refer to the software plan requirements for software validation planning and incorporation (Chapter 5 [of NPR 7150.2, NASA Software Engineering Requirements, section 5.1.1]) [detailed in SWE-102 in this Handbook]...
Examples of validation methods include but are not limited to: formal reviews, prototype demonstrations, functional demonstrations, software testing, software peer reviews/inspections of software product component, behavior in a simulated environment, acceptance testing against mathematical models, analyses, and operational environment demonstrations. Refer to the software plan requirements for software validation planning and incorporation (Chapter 5 [of NPR 7150.2, NASA Software Engineering Requirements, section 5.1.1]) [detailed in SWE-102 in this Handbook].
It is also important when the validation environment is a service performed by another organization (e.g., high-fidelity simulators or system integration labs). The basic validation process is shown below with the steps addressed by this requirement highlighted: Figure 3.1. Validation Process With Planning Steps Highlighted · Validation activities are not performed in an ad hoc manner, but are planned and captured in a validation plan document. The validation plan is typically part of a verification and validation (V&V) plan, a software V&V plan (SVVP), or is included in the Software Management / Development Plan (SMP/SDP).

items.question: How is the Heritage or legacy products section utilized in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook, and what is its purpose?
items.answer: The Heritage or legacy products section in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook describes which legacy products will be used in a project, the rationale for their use, and the analysis or testing required to ensure they perform as intended. It helps leverage existing systems to reduce costs and risks by building on past successes .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: What strategies are defined for handling risk management within the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook, and how are they integrated into the technical decision process?
items.answer: Risk management strategies in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook involve assessing risks with defined thresholds for mitigation activities and integrating risk management into the technical decision process. Regular risk assessments, communication across projects, and documentation of decisions help ensure that possible issues are proactively managed .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: How is the interfaces between technical and project management aspects coordinated as per the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook?
items.answer: The coordination between technical and project management aspects, as per the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook, involves defining roles, integrating technical requirements with the project plan for resource allocation, and updating coordination activities as needed with external stakeholders and contractors .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: Discuss the relevance of the Human Systems Integration strategy in improving project outcomes.
items.answer: The Human Systems Integration (HSI) strategy is relevant in improving project outcomes by ensuring human elements are adequately integrated into the system's design and operations, potentially increasing mission success and total system performance. Known HSI challenges and the direct impact of human performance on system operations can be identified and addressed through this strategy, leading to improved project outcomes .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: What process does the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook recommend for verifying and validating products within a project?
items.answer: The NASA Systems Engineering Handbook recommends verifying and validating products by implementing common technical processes tailored to specific project needs. This includes ensuring that necessary outputs satisfy entry and success criteria across the applicable product life cycle phases, assigning responsibilities, and involving stakeholders throughout the process .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: How does the Systems Engineering Handbook propose addressing conflicting requirements among reference documents?
items.answer: The Systems Engineering Handbook proposes addressing conflicting requirements among reference documents through an 'Order of Precedence' section that identifies which documents take precedence when requirements conflict .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: What are the key elements in assessing the Human Systems Integration (HSI) mentioned in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook?
items.answer: The key elements in assessing Human Systems Integration (HSI) include roles and responsibilities across HSI domains, coordination with stakeholders, HSI goals and deliverables for each phase, entry and exit criteria with defined metrics, standards for conducting HSI, and strategies for identifying HSI risks. The alignment strategy with the Systems Engineering Management Plan (SEMP) is also essential .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: What is the primary purpose of a Verification and Validation (V&V) Plan as outlined in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook?
items.answer: The primary purpose of a Verification and Validation (V&V) Plan, as outlined in the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook, is to identify the activities that will establish compliance with requirements (verification) and ensure that the system will meet customer expectations (validation).
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: How does the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook suggest integrating technical efforts of contractors with NASA's internal technical team?
items.answer: The NASA Systems Engineering Handbook suggests integrating the technical efforts of contractors with NASA's internal technical team by establishing technical agreements, monitoring contractor progress, handling technical work or product requirement change requests, and involving the NASA team in reviewing or controlling contractor-generated documentation .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

items.question: What are the roles of analytical tools in supporting technical effort integration according to the NASA Systems Engineering Handbook?
items.answer: Analytical tools support technical effort integration by using methods such as integrated computer-aided tool sets, work product databases, and technical management information systems. These tools facilitate the integration of technical processes and resource allocation, aiding teams in managing and tracking project progress .
items.title: NASA Systems Engineering Handbook | PDF | System | Systems Theory

results.title: SWE-029 - Validation Planning - NASA Software Engineering Handbook - Global Site
results.description: Examples of validation methods ... testing against mathematical models, analyses, and operational environment demonstrations. Refer to the software plan requirements for software validation planning and incorporation (Chapter 5 [of NPR 7150.2, NASA Software Engineering Requirements, section 5.1.1]) [detailed in SWE-102 in this Handbook]...
extra_snippets: Examples of validation methods include but are not limited to: formal reviews, prototype demonstrations, functional demonstrations, software testing, software peer reviews/inspections of software product component, behavior in a simulated environment, acceptance testing against mathematical models, analyses, and operational environment demonstrations. Refer to the software plan requirements for software validation planning and incorporation (Chapter 5 [of NPR 7150.2, NASA Software Engineering Requirements, section 5.1.1]) [detailed in SWE-102 in this Handbook].

It is also important when the validation environment is a service performed by another organization (e.g., high-fidelity simulators or system integration labs). The basic validation process is shown below with the steps addressed by this requirement highlighted: Figure 3.1. Validation Process With Planning Steps Highlighted · Validation activities are not performed in an ad hoc manner, but are planned and captured in a validation plan document. The validation plan is typically part of a verification and validation (V&V) plan, a software V&V plan (SVVP), or is included in the Software Management / Development Plan (SMP/SDP).
